title: Drama
subtitle: Literary adaptations: American 20th century, Russian classics, Shakespeare, modern, Tolkien-adjacent epic.

sections.name: I. American 20th Century
sections.desc: Hemingway, Fitzgerald, Steinbeck, plus the next-tier American novelists.

items.title: East of Eden
items.whyPriority: James Dean's first major role and his best. Steinbeck adaptation focused on the final third of the novel. Cain-and-Abel themes, father-son devastation.
items.pitch: California, 1917. A son competes for his father's love against a brother who can't lose. Watch then read; the film is a focused subset of the novel.

critics.quote: Four Oscar nominations including Best Director. Dean nominated posthumously.

items.title: The Grapes of Wrath
items.whyPriority: Ford + Steinbeck + Henry Fonda. Depression-era Joads from Oklahoma to California. Cross-listed with Ford auteur. American canon.
items.pitch: The Joad family loses their farm and migrates to California with the rest of the Dust Bowl refugees.

critics.quote: Won 2 Oscars (Best Director, Best Supporting Actress). Seven nominations.

items.title: The Great Gatsby (1974)
items.whyPriority: Coppola screenplay. Redford and Mia Farrow. Generally considered the best Gatsby adaptation — the 2013 Luhrmann is dazzling but loses the melancholy.
items.pitch: Jay Gatsby's pursuit of Daisy. The eyes of Doctor T.J. Eckleburg.

critics.quote: Critically warm. Won 2 Oscars (Costume Design, Original Song).

items.title: Of Mice and Men (1992)
items.whyPriority: Sinise directed and co-starred. Malkovich as Lennie. Steinbeck's novella unembellished.
items.pitch: Two migrant ranch hands in Depression-era California. One protects the other.

critics.quote: Critical hit. Considered the definitive Of Mice and Men adaptation.

items.title: The Old Man and the Sea (1958)
items.pitch: Spencer Tracy as Santiago. Hemingway adapted faithfully. Won Best Score (Tiomkin).

critics.quote: Critical reception mixed; Tracy and Tiomkin's score were the standouts. Three Oscar nominations.

items.title: Cat on a Hot Tin Roof
items.whyPriority: Tennessee Williams. Newman, Liz Taylor, Burl Ives. Code-era restrictions softened the source's sexuality but the family-rot core survives.
items.pitch: A wealthy Mississippi family gathers for the patriarch's birthday. Lies erupt.

critics.quote: Six Oscar nominations including Best Picture, Best Actor, Best Actress.

items.title: A Streetcar Named Desire
items.pitch: Brando's defining performance. Vivien Leigh's Blanche won the Oscar. Williams adapted by Williams.

critics.quote: Won 4 Oscars in acting categories. Twelve total nominations.

items.title: True Grit (2010)
items.whyPriority: Coens adapting Charles Portis. Hailee Steinfeld's career-launching performance. Bridges as Rooster Cogburn. The most faithful adaptation of the novel; the Wayne version is its own thing.
items.pitch: A 14-year-old hires a marshal to track down her father's killer in 1880s Indian Territory.

critics.quote: Ten Oscar nominations including Best Picture.

items.title: There Will Be Blood
items.whyPriority: PTA's masterpiece. Loose adaptation of Upton Sinclair's Oil! Day-Lewis's Daniel Plainview is one of the great modern performances. Cross-listed with PTA auteur.
items.pitch: A turn-of-the-century oil prospector's life across decades. Capitalism, religion, isolation, milkshakes.

critics.quote: Won 2 Oscars (Best Actor, Cinematography). Eight nominations including Best Picture.

sections.name: II. Russian Classics
sections.desc: Tolstoy and Dostoevsky. The film versions vary; here are the genuinely excellent ones.

items.title: Doctor Zhivago
items.whyPriority: Pasternak adapted by Robert Bolt and Lean. Sweeping epic across the Russian Revolution. Won 5 Oscars. The most accessible great Russian-novel adaptation.
items.pitch: Yuri Zhivago's life across pre-revolutionary, revolutionary, and Soviet Russia. Three women shape his life.

critics.quote: Won 5 Oscars. Ten total nominations including Best Picture.

items.title: War and Peace (1966-67, Bondarchuk)
items.whyPriority: The Soviet seven-hour adaptation. Won Best Foreign Language Film. Cast of thousands literally; the Borodino battle sequence has never been equaled in scale. A commitment, but the only film that truly captures the novel's scope.
items.pitch: Tolstoy's novel in seven hours, broken into four parts. Watch one part per session.

critics.quote: Won Best Foreign Language Film. Considered the definitive adaptation by most Tolstoy scholars.

items.title: Anna Karenina (2012, Wright)
items.whyPriority: Wright's theatrical conceit — much of the film stages Russian high society as if on a stage set — risks gimmickry but mostly works. Knightley, Jude Law (career-best), Aaron Taylor-Johnson. Tom Stoppard screenplay.
items.pitch: Tolstoy's novel through a stylized theater-as-Russia framework.

critics.quote: Polarizing. Some critics found the conceit revelatory; others distracting.

items.title: Crime and Punishment (1970, Soviet)
items.whyPriority: The most respected Crime and Punishment adaptation. Soviet, faithful, austere. Difficult to find but widely held as the only screen version that does Dostoevsky justice.
items.pitch: Dostoevsky's novel across four hours. Raskolnikov's crime, paranoia, confession.

critics.quote: Critical consensus among Dostoevsky scholars: the definitive screen adaptation.

items.title: The Tragedy of Macbeth (2021)
items.whyPriority: Joel Coen solo. Black-and-white, 1.37:1, expressionist staging. Washington and McDormand. The most formally daring Shakespeare film since Welles.
items.pitch: Macbeth as German expressionist nightmare. Scotland as sound stage. The witches as one impossibly contorted Kathryn Hunter.

critics.quote: Three Oscar nominations including Best Actor (Washington). Critical consensus as among the best Shakespeare films.

items.title: Henry V (1989)
items.whyPriority: Branagh's directorial debut. Muddier and grittier than Olivier's wartime version. The Crispin's Day speech is one of the great moments in screen Shakespeare.
items.pitch: Young king of England invades France. The Battle of Agincourt as rain-soaked muck.

critics.quote: Three Oscar nominations including Best Director and Best Actor.

items.title: Hamlet (1996, Branagh)
items.whyPriority: Full-text Hamlet, four hours, 70mm, gigantic cast (Charlton Heston, Robin Williams, Billy Crystal, Kate Winslet). Audacious, occasionally awkward, never less than committed.
items.pitch: Branagh stages every line of Hamlet. The 'too too solid flesh' soliloquy through the duel.

critics.quote: Four Oscar nominations including Best Adapted Screenplay.

items.title: Throne of Blood
items.whyPriority: Cross-listed with Kurosawa auteur and pre-1960. Macbeth in feudal Japan. T.S. Eliot called it the finest Shakespeare film ever made.
items.pitch: Macbeth transposed to feudal Japan. The arrow scene at the end is one of cinema's great climaxes.

critics.quote: T.S. Eliot praised it as the finest Shakespeare adaptation. Routinely listed among Kurosawa's masterpieces.

items.title: Ran
items.whyPriority: Kurosawa's late-career masterpiece. King Lear in feudal Japan. Cross-listed with Kurosawa auteur.
items.pitch: An aging warlord divides his kingdom between three sons. The kingdom collapses.

critics.quote: Routinely cited as one of the greatest films ever made about war and aging.

items.title: Richard III (1995)
items.whyPriority: Ian McKellen's Richard III set in fascist 1930s Britain. The conceit works because Shakespeare's text supports it. Smart, brutal.
items.pitch: Richard III in an alternate-history fascist 1930s Britain.

critics.quote: Two Oscar nominations. Critically beloved by Shakespeare scholars.

items.title: Chimes at Midnight
items.whyPriority: Welles's Falstaff film, weaving multiple Henry plays around the fat knight. Welles considered it his best film. Cross-listed with Welles auteur.
items.pitch: Welles plays Falstaff. The relationship with Prince Hal as it builds, breaks, and ends.

critics.quote: Welles considered it his best work. Critically rehabilitated as one of his masterpieces.

sections.name: IV. Modern / Contemporary
sections.desc: Atonement, Remains of the Day, Never Let Me Go territory.

items.title: Atonement
items.whyPriority: Ian McEwan adaptation. McAvoy, Knightley, young Saoirse Ronan. The Dunkirk tracking shot. Devastating final-act revelation. Cross-listed.
items.pitch: A young girl's mistaken accusation destroys lives across decades. Britain and Europe through WWII.

critics.quote: Won Best Original Score. Seven nominations including Best Picture.

items.title: The Remains of the Day
items.whyPriority: Ishiguro adaptation. Hopkins and Thompson at their finest. Restraint as art. Eight Oscar nominations.
items.pitch: An English butler reflects on his decades of service. What he repressed. Who he failed to love.

critics.quote: Eight Oscar nominations including Best Picture.

items.title: Never Let Me Go
items.whyPriority: Ishiguro adaptation. Carey Mulligan, Andrew Garfield, Keira Knightley. Sci-fi-adjacent — the world is dystopian — but the heart is the relationships. Quiet, devastating.
items.pitch: Three children grow up at an English boarding school where something is off about why they're there.

critics.quote: Critically beloved, commercially overlooked. Considered a great Ishiguro adaptation.

items.title: No Country for Old Men
items.whyPriority: Cormac McCarthy adaptation. Won Best Picture. Bardem's Anton Chigurh. Cross-listed with Coens auteur.
items.pitch: A Texas welder finds drug-deal money. A merciless killer pursues. A sheriff watches his world end.

critics.quote: Won 4 Oscars including Best Picture, Best Director, Best Adapted Screenplay, Best Supporting Actor.

items.title: The English Patient
items.whyPriority: Michael Ondaatje adaptation. Won 9 Oscars. Sweeping wartime romance with cartography, Herodotus, betrayal in the Sahara.
items.pitch: A burned man at a war's end recalls who he was, what he loved, and what he did.

critics.quote: Won 9 Oscars including Best Picture, Best Director.

items.title: Brooklyn
items.pitch: Colm Tóibín adaptation. Saoirse Ronan as a young Irish woman emigrating to 1950s Brooklyn. Quiet, lovely.

critics.quote: Three Oscar nominations including Best Picture.

sections.name: V. Tolkien-Adjacent Epic Literature
sections.desc: Adaptations of long, mythic, deeply researched literary works.

items.title: Lawrence of Arabia
items.whyPriority: T.E. Lawrence's Seven Pillars of Wisdom. Lean's masterpiece. O'Toole's Lawrence is one of the great screen performances. Won 7 Oscars. The closest cinema has come to true epic.
items.pitch: British officer's role in the Arab Revolt during WWI. Four hours of desert, mythology, and self-destruction.

critics.quote: Won 7 Oscars including Best Picture, Best Director.

items.title: The Last Samurai
items.whyPriority: Cruise as a U.S. Army officer who becomes immersed in samurai culture during the Meiji Restoration. Watanabe's career-launching performance for Western audiences.
items.pitch: 1870s Japan. The samurai class is being abolished by modernization. An American officer sent to train the new army is captured.

critics.quote: Four Oscar nominations including Best Supporting Actor (Watanabe).

items.title: Master and Commander
items.whyPriority: Patrick O'Brian adaptation. Crowe as Captain Jack Aubrey. Naval procedural meets character study. Underseen by general audiences but treasured by literature people.
items.pitch: Napoleonic-era British frigate hunts a French privateer around South America.

critics.quote: Ten Oscar nominations. Won 2 (Cinematography, Sound Editing).
